tools.name: add-to-context
tools.description:
Adds the object to the chat context.
Use this it for objects that are useful long-term for the conversation.

obj.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: remove-from-context
tools.description:
Removes the object from the chat context.
Use this it for objects that are no longer useful for the conversation.

obj.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: load-object
tools.description:
Loads the object or relation content.
Can load multiple objects at at time.
Use the to read the data when you have a DXN.
Call this tool with an array of one or more DXNs or object IDs.
When use see a reference ({ '/': 'dxn:...' }), you can call this function to load the object.
Note that returned data is only a snapshot in time, and might have changed since the object was last loaded.

items.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: create-object
tools.description:
Creates a new object and adds it to the current space.
Get the schema from the schema-list tool and ensure that the data matches the corresponding schema.
References are provided in the following format: { "/": "dxn:..." }.
Reference examples: { "/": "dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }, { "/": "dxn:queue:data:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }

tools.name: delete-object
tools.description: Deletes the object.

obj.description:

- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: update-object

obj.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:

- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

tools.name: query

Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

Use the to read the data when you have a DXN.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>
List all available schemas. Tell me what typenames are available.

content.text: The user wants to see all available schemas and their typenames.

key.description: Unique registration key for the blueprint

description.description: Description of the blueprint's purpose and functionality

instructions.description: Instructions that guide the AI assistant's behavior and responses

tools.description: Array of tools that the AI assistant can use when this blueprint is active

agentCanEnable.description: Whether an agent is allowed to auto-enable this blueprint in a conversation.

key.description: Unique registration key for the blueprint

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

response.delta: me organize the available typenames from the schema list.

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

|

accepts additional properties beyond just an `id`. |
| 2 | `org
.dxos.type.blueprint` | Defines AI assistant blueprints with instructions, tools, and M

CP server configurations. |
| 3 | `org.dxos.type.feed` | Represents a data feed with a name
.function` | Defines functions with input/output schemas, versioning, and service

bindings. |
| 5 | `org.dxos.type.organization` | An organization with name, description, status
with full name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, URLs, job title

Defines prompts with input/output JSON schemas, instructions, blueprints, and context. |
| 8 | `org.dxos.type.tag` | A
simple tag with a label and optional hue (color). Used

for tagging/categorizing objects. |
| 9 | `org.dxos.type.view` | Defines views with queries

and projections (field configurations) for displaying data. |

These
are the 9 typenames currently available in the space. The most commonly used ones

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>
List all available schemas. Tell me what typenames are available.

content.text: The user wants to see all available schemas and their typenames.

key.description: Unique registration key for the blueprint

description.description: Description of the blueprint's purpose and functionality

instructions.description: Instructions that guide the AI assistant's behavior and responses

tools.description: Array of tools that the AI assistant can use when this blueprint is active

agentCanEnable.description: Whether an agent is allowed to auto-enable this blueprint in a conversation.

key.description: Unique registration key for the blueprint

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

content.text: Let me organize the available typenames from the schema list.

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | `com.example.type.expando` | A flexible/dynamic object type that accepts additional properties beyond just an `id`. |
| 2 | `org.dxos.type.blueprint` | Defines AI assistant blueprints with instructions, tools, and MCP server configurations. |
| 3 | `org.dxos.type.feed` | Represents a data feed with a name, kind, and namespace (data or trace). |
| 4 | `org.dxos.type.function` | Defines functions with input/output schemas, versioning, and service bindings. |
| 5 | `org.dxos.type.organization` | An organization with name, description, status (prospect/qualified/active/commit/reject), image, website, and location. |
| 6 | `org.dxos.type.person` | A person/contact with full name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, URLs, job title, organization reference, birthday, and more. |
| 7 | `org.dxos.type.prompt` | Defines prompts with input/output JSON schemas, instructions, blueprints, and context. |
| 8 | `org.dxos.type.tag` | A simple tag with a label and optional hue (color). Used for tagging/categorizing objects. |
| 9 | `org.dxos.type.view` | Defines views with queries and projections (field configurations) for displaying data. |

These are the 9 typenames currently available in the space. The most commonly used ones for data entry would be **Organization**, **Person**, **Tag**, and **Expando** (for freeform data). The others are more system/configuration-oriented (blueprints, functions, prompts, feeds, views).

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>
List all available schemas. Tell me what typenames are available.

content.text: The user wants to see all available schemas and their typenames.

key.description: Unique registration key for the blueprint

description.description: Description of the blueprint's purpose and functionality

instructions.description: Instructions that guide the AI assistant's behavior and responses

tools.description: Array of tools that the AI assistant can use when this blueprint is active

agentCanEnable.description: Whether an agent is allowed to auto-enable this blueprint in a conversation.

key.description: Unique registration key for the blueprint

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

content.text: Let me organize the available typenames from the schema list.

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | `com.example.type.expando` | A flexible/dynamic object type that accepts additional properties beyond just an `id`. |
| 2 | `org.dxos.type.blueprint` | Defines AI assistant blueprints with instructions, tools, and MCP server configurations. |
| 3 | `org.dxos.type.feed` | Represents a data feed with a name, kind, and namespace (data or trace). |
| 4 | `org.dxos.type.function` | Defines functions with input/output schemas, versioning, and service bindings. |
| 5 | `org.dxos.type.organization` | An organization with name, description, status (prospect/qualified/active/commit/reject), image, website, and location. |
| 6 | `org.dxos.type.person` | A person/contact with full name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, URLs, job title, organization reference, birthday, and more. |
| 7 | `org.dxos.type.prompt` | Defines prompts with input/output JSON schemas, instructions, blueprints, and context. |
| 8 | `org.dxos.type.tag` | A simple tag with a label and optional hue (color). Used for tagging/categorizing objects. |
| 9 | `org.dxos.type.view` | Defines views with queries and projections (field configurations) for displaying data. |

These are the 9 typenames currently available in the space. The most commonly used ones for data entry would be **Organization**, **Person**, **Tag**, and **Expando** (for freeform data). The others are more system/configuration-oriented (blueprints, functions, prompts, feeds, views).

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

## Context Objects

<object>
  <dxn>dxn:echo:BDJXMULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTA8</dxn>
  <typename>org.dxos.type.organization</typename>
</object>
Create an organization called "Cyberdyne Systems".

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query

Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.

Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">

    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>
List all available schemas. Tell me what typenames are available.

content.text: The user wants to see all available schemas and their typenames.

key.description: Unique registration key for the blueprint

description.description: Description of the blueprint's purpose and functionality

instructions.description: Instructions that guide the AI assistant's behavior and responses

tools.description: Array of tools that the AI assistant can use when this blueprint is active

agentCanEnable.description: Whether an agent is allowed to auto-enable this blueprint in a conversation.

key.description: Unique registration key for the blueprint

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

content.text: Let me organize the available typenames from the schema list.

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | `com.example.type.expando` | A flexible/dynamic object type that accepts additional properties beyond just an `id`. |
| 2 | `org.dxos.type.blueprint` | Defines AI assistant blueprints with instructions, tools, and MCP server configurations. |
| 3 | `org.dxos.type.feed` | Represents a data feed with a name, kind, and namespace (data or trace). |
| 4 | `org.dxos.type.function` | Defines functions with input/output schemas, versioning, and service bindings. |
| 5 | `org.dxos.type.organization` | An organization with name, description, status (prospect/qualified/active/commit/reject), image, website, and location. |
| 6 | `org.dxos.type.person` | A person/contact with full name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, URLs, job title, organization reference, birthday, and more. |
| 7 | `org.dxos.type.prompt` | Defines prompts with input/output JSON schemas, instructions, blueprints, and context. |
| 8 | `org.dxos.type.tag` | A simple tag with a label and optional hue (color). Used for tagging/categorizing objects. |
| 9 | `org.dxos.type.view` | Defines views with queries and projections (field configurations) for displaying data. |

These are the 9 typenames currently available in the space. The most commonly used ones for data entry would be **Organization**, **Person**, **Tag**, and **Expando** (for freeform data). The others are more system/configuration-oriented (blueprints, functions, prompts, feeds, views).

content.content: Create an organization called "DXOS" and a person named "Alice".

content.text: The user wants me to create two objects: an organization called "DXOS" and a person named "Alice". Let me create both simultaneously since they're independent.

response.delta:
MULPW5FB55UZ7KP5HLDHVTXUTHE2U:01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTK9`)

Alice to the DXOS organization, or add any additional details to either record?

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>

</blueprint>

description.description: Description of the blueprint's purpose and functionality

instructions.description: Instructions that guide the AI assistant's behavior and responses

agentCanEnable.description: Whether an agent is allowed to auto-enable this blueprint in a conversation.

key.description: Unique registration key for the blueprint

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

content.text: Let me organize the available typenames from the schema list.

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | `com.example.type.expando` | A flexible/dynamic object type that accepts additional properties beyond just an `id`. |
| 2 | `org.dxos.type.blueprint` | Defines AI assistant blueprints with instructions, tools, and MCP server configurations. |
| 3 | `org.dxos.type.feed` | Represents a data feed with a name, kind, and namespace (data or trace). |
| 4 | `org.dxos.type.function` | Defines functions with input/output schemas, versioning, and service bindings. |
| 5 | `org.dxos.type.organization` | An organization with name, description, status (prospect/qualified/active/commit/reject), image, website, and location. |
| 6 | `org.dxos.type.person` | A person/contact with full name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, URLs, job title, organization reference, birthday, and more. |
| 7 | `org.dxos.type.prompt` | Defines prompts with input/output JSON schemas, instructions, blueprints, and context. |
| 8 | `org.dxos.type.tag` | A simple tag with a label and optional hue (color). Used for tagging/categorizing objects. |
| 9 | `org.dxos.type.view` | Defines views with queries and projections (field configurations) for displaying data. |

These are the 9 typenames currently available in the space. The most commonly used ones for data entry would be **Organization**, **Person**, **Tag**, and **Expando** (for freeform data). The others are more system/configuration-oriented (blueprints, functions, prompts, feeds, views).

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.text: Now I have a comprehensive picture of DXOS. Let me compile this research into a thorough summary.

Here is a comprehensive research summary on **DXOS**:

---

# DXOS — The Decentralized Operating System

## Overview
DXOS is the Decentralized Operating System — a platform for building fully decentralized applications.
It is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers.
DXOS was founded in 2021
and
is headquartered in New York, NY.
## Mission & Philosophy
The mission at DXOS is to help developers build privacy-preserving, self-sovereign software.

The team is setting a new standard for how modern software is built by reducing reliance on centralized servers and focusing on scalable peer-to-peer data networks. Their framework empowers anyone to create real-time collaborative tools within a privacy-focused, local-first workspace that is enhanced by AI capabilities.

DXOS is a developer of a decentralized platform designed to create the first functionally and commercially competitive, fully decentralized alternative to the corporate cloud. The company offers a network operating system, infrastructure platform, programming framework, and toolchain.
This enables businesses to develop privacy-preserving applications for its users and a fairer marketplace for open-source developers.
## Company & Community
Braneframe is a Web3 company building peer-to-peer privacy-preserving applications built on open-source protocols. It is a leading project in the DXOS (Decentralized Operating System) ecosystem.
DXOS is classified as a nonprofit type in the Technology, Information and Internet industry with 11-50 employees.
The community spans 8 cities worldwide, with 30+ contributors and 200+ community members.
### Funding & Investors
Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States) have invested in DXOS.
## Core SDK Components

The DXOS SDK includes several technologies that work together:
- **ECHO** – Peer-to-peer database and reactive state container for offline-first, real-time, collaborative apps.
- **HALO** – Auth, identity & contact management for decentralized apps.
- **MESH** – Networking transports used by ECHO & HALO.
- **EDGE** – Web services running on Cloudflare workers, providing sync, backup, compute, etc.
- **App Framework** – Build modular applications based around plugins & capabilities, such as Composer.
### ECHO (Database & Sync)
ECHO is an open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage.
DXOS leverages Automerge — a powerful CRDT that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.
### HALO (Identity)
HALO seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.
### MESH (Networking)
MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.
Data is automatically synchronized across networked devices using WebRTC for cloudless peer-to-peer networking.
## Architecture & How It Works
The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell. Everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer.
User identity, data storage, synchronization and networking are all handled natively using the DXOS client API.
There are still signaling servers for peer discovery, but all data exchange happens peer-to-peer, and no servers have access to the data.

Key features include: comprehensive SDK for building local-first, peer-to-peer applications; support for offline functionality with automatic synchronization upon reconnection; emphasis on user privacy by eliminating the need for central servers; and support for integration of local AI models for enhanced functionality.
## Developer Experience
DXOS works in any Node.js or Browser environment, with both a TypeScript API and a React API.

DXOS is a developer platform for building beautiful, local-first, collaborative applications. It consists of three main components that work together, wrapped in a set of SDKs (currently React and TypeScript) that enable developers to deliver sophisticated applications quickly.
DXOS recommends Vite as the bundler. Vite requires a plugin in order to serve the WebAssembly modules.
## Composer — The Flagship Application
DXOS is also the home of Composer — an extensible IDE, built for both real time and async collaboration.
Composer helps teams build and organize knowledge, and create customizable AI-driven workflows — all in one modular, composable workspace.
Composer blends the power of popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) inside of a collaborative local-first workspace.

Capabilities include:

-
Realtime collaboration inside of a modern markdown editor.
Threaded comments across multiple document formats.
Custom plugins to enhance functionality.
Custom schemas to work with structured data.
A local-first spreadsheet that supports custom functions.
Machine learning to build agentic workflows that enhance team productivity while protecting intellectual property.
### Plugin Architecture

Composer is built with extensibility at its core. Everything within the application is built using the same App Framework APIs, from the UI down to data and business logic — anything in Composer can be customized and enhanced.
### Privacy Model
Composer is built on the peer-to-peer protocols of DXOS which are designed to be private by default. The data is stored on your device and only shared with others you explicitly invite.
Due to the CRDT-based implementation there's no need for a server other than for backup. DXOS provides backup services if you want them, but you can also host your own.
## DXOS Agents
DXOS is launching a fully-managed service that runs your agents without having to worry about the underlying infrastructure and uptime. With a single click, you can start an agent in seconds.
Agents are just like any other peer, but are assumed to have more uptime on the network. They should typically be running on servers or desktop machines which are always plugged in.
Anyone can run a regular peer on any machine they want and that would qualify as an agent. They also offer an open-source CLI tool which can run the agent on any machine from the command line.

The vision is to enable significant savings through the interoperability of DXOS applications. Local-first apps can share the same agent and data storage, maintaining data privacy without extra charges for each additional app. Similar to iCloud storage, which allows various applications to write to a single data storage layer, this storage belongs entirely to you and can be run anywhere.
## Open Source & GitHub
DXOS has 64 repositories available on GitHub.
The project is
a TypeScript implementation of the DXOS protocols, SDK, toolchain and Composer.
The codebase is licensed under MIT, and
the main dxos/dxos repository was updated as recently as February 13, 2026.

## Summary

DXOS represents a bold vision for the future of software development — one where applications are local-first, peer-to-peer, privacy-preserving, and free from dependence on centralized cloud infrastructure. Through its SDK (ECHO, HALO, MESH, EDGE) and its flagship app Composer, DXOS provides developers with a comprehensive toolkit for building collaborative, real-time applications that put data ownership back into the hands of users. The platform leverages CRDTs (via Automerge), WebRTC, and decentralized identity to achieve seamless collaboration without traditional server backends.

params.success:
Comprehensive research on DXOS (Decentralized Operating System) has been completed. DXOS is an open-source framework founded in 2021 and headquartered in New York, NY, for building real-time, collaborative, local-first web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer without centralized servers. Key findings include:

**Core Technology Stack:**
- ECHO: Peer-to-peer database with CRDT-based sync (uses Automerge)
- HALO: Decentralized identity management via public/private key pairs
- MESH: Peer-to-peer networking via WebRTC
- EDGE: Cloudflare Workers-based web services for sync, backup, compute
- App Framework: Plugin-based modular application architecture

**Flagship Product — Composer:**
An extensible, local-first collaborative workspace (described as a "Super App") supporting docs, sheets, tables, sketches, custom plugins, custom schemas, and AI-driven agentic workflows. Privacy by default with data stored on-device.

**Agents:**
DXOS offers a managed agent service — always-on peers that provide data availability when other peers are offline, with an open-source CLI for self-hosting.

**Company Details:**
- Founded: 2021
- HQ: New York, NY
- Type: Nonprofit / Open Source (MIT license)
- Employees: 11-50
- Investors: Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, PL Capital
- Community: 30+ contributors, 200+ community members across 8 cities
- GitHub: 64 repositories, TypeScript-based, actively maintained (last update Feb 2026)

**Key Differentiators:**
- No centralized servers needed (only signaling servers for peer discovery)
- CRDT-based conflict-free real-time collaboration
- Offline-first with automatic sync on reconnection
- User-owned data with no lock-in
- Local AI model integration support
- React and TypeScript SDKs available

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

</object>

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.

  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.

content.text: Let me summarize the available typenames from the schemas.

Here are all the available typenames in your space:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **`com.example.type.expando`** | A flexible/generic object type that allows arbitrary additional properties. |
| 2 | **`org.dxos.type.blueprint`** | Defines AI assistant blueprints with instructions, tools, and MCP server configurations. |
| 3 | **`org.dxos.type.feed`** | A feed/stream container (e.g., for data or trace items). |
| 4 | **`org.dxos.type.function`** | Represents a function with input/output schemas, versioning, and bindings. |
| 5 | **`org.dxos.type.organization`** | An organization record with name, description, status (prospect/qualified/active/commit/reject), website, image, and location. |
| 6 | **`org.dxos.type.person`** | A person/contact record with full name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, URLs, job title, organization reference, birthday, and more. |
| 7 | **`org.dxos.type.prompt`** | A prompt definition with input/output JSON schemas, instructions, blueprints, and context. |
| 8 | **`org.dxos.type.tag`** | A simple tag with a label and optional hue (color), used to tag other objects. |
| 9 | **`org.dxos.type.trigger`** | Defines triggers (email, queue, subscription, timer, webhook) that invoke functions. |
| 10 | **`org.dxos.type.view`** | A saved view/query with projection settings for displaying filtered data. |

These are the 10 schema typenames currently available. The most commonly used ones for data entry would be **Person**, **Organization**, **Tag**, and **Expando** (for freeform data). The others are more system/configuration-oriented (blueprints, functions, triggers, views, prompts, feeds).

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>
List all available schemas. Tell me what typenames are available.

content.text: The user wants to see all available schemas and their typenames.

key.description: Unique registration key for the blueprint

description.description: Description of the blueprint's purpose and functionality

instructions.description: Instructions that guide the AI assistant's behavior and responses

tools.description: Array of tools that the AI assistant can use when this blueprint is active

agentCanEnable.description: Whether an agent is allowed to auto-enable this blueprint in a conversation.

key.description: Unique registration key for the blueprint

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

content.text: Let me summarize the available typenames from the schemas.

Here are all the available typenames in your space:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **`com.example.type.expando`** | A flexible/generic object type that allows arbitrary additional properties. |
| 2 | **`org.dxos.type.blueprint`** | Defines AI assistant blueprints with instructions, tools, and MCP server configurations. |
| 3 | **`org.dxos.type.feed`** | A feed/stream container (e.g., for data or trace items). |
| 4 | **`org.dxos.type.function`** | Represents a function with input/output schemas, versioning, and bindings. |
| 5 | **`org.dxos.type.organization`** | An organization record with name, description, status (prospect/qualified/active/commit/reject), website, image, and location. |
| 6 | **`org.dxos.type.person`** | A person/contact record with full name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, URLs, job title, organization reference, birthday, and more. |
| 7 | **`org.dxos.type.prompt`** | A prompt definition with input/output JSON schemas, instructions, blueprints, and context. |
| 8 | **`org.dxos.type.tag`** | A simple tag with a label and optional hue (color), used to tag other objects. |
| 9 | **`org.dxos.type.trigger`** | Defines triggers (email, queue, subscription, timer, webhook) that invoke functions. |
| 10 | **`org.dxos.type.view`** | A saved view/query with projection settings for displaying filtered data. |

These are the 10 schema typenames currently available. The most commonly used ones for data entry would be **Person**, **Organization**, **Tag**, and **Expando** (for freeform data). The others are more system/configuration-oriented (blueprints, functions, triggers, views, prompts, feeds).

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

content.text: Let me summarize the available typenames from the schemas.

Here are all the available typenames in your space:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **`com.example.type.expando`** | A flexible/generic object type that allows arbitrary additional properties. |
| 2 | **`org.dxos.type.blueprint`** | Defines AI assistant blueprints with instructions, tools, and MCP server configurations. |
| 3 | **`org.dxos.type.feed`** | A feed/stream container (e.g., for data or trace items). |
| 4 | **`org.dxos.type.function`** | Represents a function with input/output schemas, versioning, and bindings. |
| 5 | **`org.dxos.type.organization`** | An organization record with name, description, status (prospect/qualified/active/commit/reject), website, image, and location. |
| 6 | **`org.dxos.type.person`** | A person/contact record with full name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, URLs, job title, organization reference, birthday, and more. |
| 7 | **`org.dxos.type.prompt`** | A prompt definition with input/output JSON schemas, instructions, blueprints, and context. |
| 8 | **`org.dxos.type.tag`** | A simple tag with a label and optional hue (color), used to tag other objects. |
| 9 | **`org.dxos.type.trigger`** | Defines triggers (email, queue, subscription, timer, webhook) that invoke functions. |
| 10 | **`org.dxos.type.view`** | A saved view/query with projection settings for displaying filtered data. |

These are the 10 schema typenames currently available. The most commonly used ones for data entry would be **Person**, **Organization**, **Tag**, and **Expando** (for freeform data). The others are more system/configuration-oriented (blueprints, functions, triggers, views, prompts, feeds).

content.text: Now I have comprehensive information about DXOS. Let me compile a thorough research summary.

Here is a comprehensive research summary on **DXOS (Decentralized Operating System)**:

---

## Overview
DXOS is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers.
DXOS is the Decentralized Operating System — a platform for building fully decentralized applications.

The company is described as a developer of a decentralized platform designed to create the first functionally and commercially competitive, fully decentralized alternative to the corporate cloud, offering a network operating system, infrastructure platform, programming framework, and toolchain, enabling businesses to develop privacy-preserving applications for its users and a fairer marketplace for open-source developers.

## Company Details

- **Founded:**
DXOS was founded in 2021.
- **Headquarters:**
DXOS is headquartered in New York, NY.
- **Type:**
DXOS is a Private company.
- **Industry:**
DXOS's primary industry is Software Development Applications.
- **Team Size:**
11-50 employees.
- **Organization:**
Listed as a Nonprofit type on LinkedIn.
- **Investors:**
Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States) have invested in DXOS.
- **Ecosystem:**

Braneframe is a Web3 company building peer-to-peer privacy-preserving applications built on open-source protocols. It is a leading project in the DXOS (Decentralized Operating System) ecosystem, whose participants are working toward an alternative to the tech giant-dominated public cloud.
## Mission & Philosophy
DXOS's mission is to help developers build privacy-preserving, self-sovereign software.

They are setting a new standard for how modern software is built by reducing reliance on centralized servers and focusing on scalable peer-to-peer data networks. Their framework empowers anyone to create real-time collaborative tools within a privacy-focused, local-first workspace that is enhanced by AI capabilities.

## Core SDK Components

The DXOS SDK is composed of several key technologies that work together:

### 1. ECHO (Database & State Management)
ECHO is a peer-to-peer database and reactive state container for offline-first, real-time, collaborative apps.
ECHO is an open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage.
ECHO is based on the Automerge CRDT, so both real time and offline collaboration is possible without manual conflict resolution.
### 2. HALO (Identity & Access Control)
HALO provides auth, identity, and contact management for decentralized apps.
HALO seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.
### 3. MESH (Networking)
MESH provides networking transports used by ECHO and HALO.
MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.
### 4. EDGE (Web Services)
EDGE provides web services running on Cloudflare workers, providing sync, backup, compute, etc.
### 5. App Framework
The App Framework enables developers to build modular applications based around plugins and capabilities, such as Composer.
## Technical Architecture
DXOS leverages Automerge, a powerful CRDT that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.
The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell. Everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer.

When different devices come online, they discover relevant peers in their spaces through a signaling service. Once discovered, peers exchange offers to establish peer-to-peer WebRTC connections and begin replicating user data that way.
There are still signaling servers for peer discovery, but all data exchange happens peer-to-peer, and no servers have access to the data.

ECHO uses Epochs via a low level MigrationBuilder API to assist with coordinating changes in this decentralized environment. Epochs are a way to manage the ever-growing history of changes to the data in the underlying Automerge CRDT of ECHO.
## Developer Experience
DXOS works in any Node.js or Browser environment. There is a TypeScript API and a React API.

DXOS is a developer platform for building beautiful, local-first, collaborative applications. It consists of three main components that work together, wrapped in a set of SDKs (currently, React and TypeScript) that enable developers to deliver sophisticated applications quickly.
## Composer — The Flagship Application
DXOS is also the home of Composer — an extensible IDE, built for both real time and async collaboration.
Composer is a collaborative productivity app where developers can build and organize knowledge, extend with custom data and UI, and run private AI against their knowledge locally.
Composer blends the power of popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) inside of a collaborative local-first workspace.
Composer is built on the peer-to-peer protocols of DXOS which are designed to be private by default. The data is stored on your device and only shared with others you explicitly invite.
Everything in the app is provided by plugins. This has been a helpful driver of clean separation of concerns.
## Agents (Managed Service)

DXOS is launching a fully-managed service to host agents. DXOS is launching a fully-managed service that runs your agents without having to worry about the underlying infrastructure and uptime. With a single click, you can start an agent in seconds.

When all peers go offline, it's impossible for new peers to replicate — this is where agents come in. Agents are just like any other peer, but are assumed to have more uptime on the network. They should typically be running on servers or desktop machines which are always plugged in.

Their vision is to enable significant savings through the interoperability of DXOS applications. Local-first apps can share the same agent and data storage, maintaining data privacy without extra charges for each additional app. Similar to iCloud storage, which allows various applications to write to a single data storage layer, this storage belongs entirely to you and can be run anywhere.
## Open Source & Community
DXOS has 64 repositories available on GitHub.
The platform is open-source under the MIT license.
The DXOS Community is made of a diverse group of open source developers from all over the world.
DXOS was created by a small core team of developers from many countries. Their goal is to enable developers to quickly and easily create powerful collaborative, interoperable applications.

## Key Differentiators from Traditional SaaS

- **Local-first:** Data resides on the user's device, not on cloud servers
- **Peer-to-peer:** Communication happens directly between clients via WebRTC
- **Privacy-preserving:** No central servers have access to user data
- **Offline-capable:**
Supports offline functionality with automatic synchronization upon reconnection

- **CRDT-based:** Conflict-free data synchronization via Automerge
- **Decentralized identity:** Managed via public/private key pairs rather than centralized auth

---

DXOS represents a significant effort in the **local-first software** movement, providing a comprehensive platform that aims to give developers all the tools needed to build collaborative applications without relying on traditional centralized cloud infrastructure, while preserving user privacy and data sovereignty.

## Overview

DXOS is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers. It is described as a platform for building fully decentralized applications, aiming to create the first functionally and commercially competitive, fully decentralized alternative to the corporate cloud.

## Company Details

- **Founded:** 2021
- **Headquarters:** New York, NY
- **Type:** Private company (listed as Nonprofit on LinkedIn)
- **Industry:** Software Development Applications
- **Team Size:** 11-50 employees
- **Investors:** Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States)
- **Ecosystem:** Part of the broader Protocol Labs network; Braneframe is a leading project in the DXOS ecosystem

## Mission & Philosophy

DXOS's mission is to help developers build privacy-preserving, self-sovereign software. They aim to set a new standard for how modern software is built by reducing reliance on centralized servers and focusing on scalable peer-to-peer data networks.

## Core SDK Components

### 1. ECHO (Database & State Management)
A peer-to-peer database and reactive state container for offline-first, real-time, collaborative apps. It incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage, based on the Automerge CRDT.

### 2. HALO (Identity & Access Control)
Auth, identity, and contact management for decentralized apps. It manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.

### 3. MESH (Networking)
Networking transports used by ECHO and HALO. MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.

### 4. EDGE (Web Services)
Web services running on Cloudflare workers, providing sync, backup, compute, etc.

### 5. App Framework
Build modular applications based around plugins and capabilities, such as Composer.

## Technical Architecture

- **CRDT-based:** DXOS leverages Automerge, a powerful CRDT that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict
- **Peer-to-peer:** The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell; everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer
- **WebRTC networking:** Peers discover each other through signaling servers, then exchange offers to establish peer-to-peer WebRTC connections and begin replicating user data
- **Epochs:** ECHO uses Epochs to manage the ever-growing history of changes in the underlying Automerge CRDT
- **SDKs:** TypeScript API and React API; works in Node.js and Browser environments

## Composer — The Flagship Application

Composer is an extensible IDE built for both real-time and async collaboration. It blends popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) inside a collaborative local-first workspace. Data is stored on the device and only shared with explicitly invited collaborators. Everything in the app is provided by plugins, serving as a clean separation of concerns. Composer also supports running private AI against user knowledge locally.

## Agents (Managed Service)

DXOS launched a fully-managed service to host agents — peers that maintain high uptime on the network to ensure data availability when other peers are offline. The vision is to enable savings through DXOS application interoperability, where local-first apps can share the same agent and data storage, maintaining data privacy without extra charges.

## Open Source & Community

- 64 repositories on GitHub
- Open-source under the MIT license
- Created by a small core team of developers from many countries
- Active community of open source developers worldwide
- TypeScript implementation of the DXOS protocols, SDK, toolchain, and Composer

## Key Differentiators from Traditional SaaS

- **Local-first:** Data resides on the user's device, not cloud servers
- **Peer-to-peer:** Direct communication between clients via WebRTC
- **Privacy-preserving:** No central servers have access to user data
- **Offline-capable:** Supports offline functionality with automatic synchronization upon reconnection
- **CRDT-based:** Conflict-free data synchronization via Automerge
- **Decentralized identity:** Managed via public/private key pairs rather than centralized auth

DXOS represents a significant effort in the local-first software movement, providing a comprehensive platform that aims to give developers all the tools needed to build collaborative applications without relying on traditional centralized cloud infrastructure, while preserving user privacy and data sovereignty.
